title: Tram number 28, second from the back
summary: On Lisbon's old tram 28, a tourist asks the conductor why he always stands at the back. The answer is not what she expects.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Excuse me, sir. Can I ask you a question? I have ridden this tram three times this week.

body.speaker: Mateus
body.text: Three times! You like the tram.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: I do. But I noticed something. You always stand at the same place. Second from the back, on the right side. Why?

body.speaker: Mateus
body.text: You are a careful watcher. Most tourists only watch the city.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: I am a teacher. I watch people. So — why that spot?

body.speaker: Mateus
body.text: From there, I can see the door, the driver's mirror, and the steep hill at Graça. Three things at once. If I stand at the front, I cannot see the back door. If I stand at the back, I cannot see the driver.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: That makes sense. But you are not just looking at three things. You are also standing very still. Even when the tram shakes.

body.speaker: Mateus
body.text: That is the second reason. If I move, an old woman next to me will move too. She does not want to move. If I am still, she is still. My body is her handrail.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Your body is her handrail. I have never heard a job described like that.

body.speaker: Mateus
body.text: I learned it from my uncle. He worked on this tram for thirty-one years. He said, 'A good conductor is furniture that thinks.'

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Furniture that thinks. I am going to write that down.

body.speaker: Mateus
body.text: Write it down, but also try it. Stand still for one stop. You will feel how many people are leaning on you.

annotations.meaningVi: xe điện (chạy trên đường ray giữa phố)
người phụ trách trên xe / kiểm soát viên
vị trí thứ hai từ phía sau đếm lên
rung lắc (động từ shake → shakes ở ngôi thứ ba)
dựa vào bạn / tựa vào bạn — cụm động từ: lean on someone
hợp lý / có lý — cụm cố định: 'make sense' = nghe có lý
ghi lại điều đó — cụm động từ: write down

grammarNotes.title: Adverbs of frequency: 'always', 'never', 'often'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Adverb of frequency (always, often, sometimes, rarely, never) thường đứng trước động từ chính, nhưng đứng sau động từ 'be'. Trong bài: 'You always stand at the same place' (đứng trước động từ 'stand'). 'I have never heard a job described like that' (đứng giữa trợ động từ 'have' và động từ chính 'heard'). Quan sát vị trí: subject + adverb + verb.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Adverbs of frequency (always, often, sometimes, rarely, never) usually sit before the main verb, but after the verb 'be'. From the dialogue: 'You always stand at the same place' (before the main verb). 'I have never heard a job described like that' (between the auxiliary 'have' and the main verb 'heard'). Pattern: subject + adverb + verb.

translationVi:
Chloe: Xin lỗi anh. Tôi hỏi một câu được không? Tôi đã đi chiếc xe điện này ba lần tuần này rồi.
Mateus: Ba lần! Cô thích xe điện đấy.
Chloe: Vâng. Nhưng tôi để ý một điều. Anh luôn đứng cùng một chỗ. Thứ hai từ phía sau, bên phải. Tại sao vậy?
Mateus: Cô là người quan sát kỹ. Đa số khách du lịch chỉ ngắm thành phố thôi.
Chloe: Tôi là giáo viên. Tôi hay quan sát người. Vậy — tại sao chỗ đó?
Mateus: Từ đó, tôi nhìn được cửa, gương của bác tài, và con dốc ở Graça. Ba thứ cùng lúc. Nếu tôi đứng phía trước, tôi không thấy cửa sau. Nếu đứng phía sau, tôi không thấy bác tài.
Chloe: Có lý. Nhưng anh không chỉ nhìn ba thứ. Anh còn đứng rất yên. Cả khi xe rung lắc.
Mateus: Đó là lý do thứ hai. Nếu tôi nhúc nhích, bà cụ đứng cạnh tôi cũng sẽ nhúc nhích. Bà ấy không muốn di chuyển. Nếu tôi yên, bà cũng yên. Cơ thể tôi là tay vịn của bà.
Chloe: Cơ thể anh là tay vịn của bà ấy. Tôi chưa bao giờ nghe ai mô tả công việc kiểu đó.
Mateus: Tôi học điều đó từ chú tôi. Ông làm trên chiếc xe điện này ba mươi mốt năm. Ông nói: 'Một người phụ xe giỏi là món đồ nội thất biết nghĩ.'
Chloe: Đồ nội thất biết nghĩ. Tôi sẽ ghi lại câu đó.
Mateus: Ghi lại đi, nhưng cũng thử xem. Đứng yên một trạm. Cô sẽ cảm nhận được có bao nhiêu người đang tựa vào mình.

questions.prompt: How many times has Chloe ridden the tram this week?
questions.explanation: 'I have ridden this tram three times this week.'

options: Second from the back, on the right side
questions.explanation: 'Second from the back, on the right side.'

questions.prompt: What three things can he see from that spot?
options: The door, the driver's mirror, and the steep hill at Graça
Only the door and the driver

questions.prompt: Why does he stand still even when the tram shakes?
options: Because an old woman next to him relies on his body as a handrail.

questions.prompt: Who taught Mateus about this work?
options: His uncle, who worked on the tram for thirty-one years

questions.prompt: What did the uncle call a good conductor?
What does Mateus invite Chloe to do?
options: Stand still for one stop and feel how many people lean on her
questions.explanation: 'Stand still for one stop. You will feel how many people are leaning on you.'

questions.prompt: What does 'My body is her handrail' mean?
options: He stays still so the old woman has something steady to lean on.
His body is made of metal.
He is the same height as a handrail.
questions.explanation: Metaphor explained by 'if I am still, she is still.'

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the dialogue?
questions.explanation: Chloe is curious; Mateus is calm and proud of his work.
questions.hint: Notice how both speakers treat the subject.

options: A job that looks like just standing around can actually be careful, invisible care for strangers.
Trams in Lisbon are too dangerous for old people.
questions.explanation: The hidden purpose behind Mateus's spot.

cloze.template:
Chloe: Excuse me, sir. Can I ask you a question? I have ridden this tram three {{b1}} this week.
Mateus: Three times! You like the tram.
Chloe: I do. But I noticed something. You always stand at the same place. Second from the {{b2}}, on the right side. Why?
Mateus: You are a careful watcher. Most tourists only watch the city.
Chloe: I am a {{b3}}. I watch people. So — why that spot?
Mateus: From there, I can see the door, the driver's mirror, and the {{b4}} hill at Graça. Three things at once. If I stand at the front, I cannot see the back door. If I stand at the back, I cannot see the driver.
Chloe: That makes sense. But you are not just looking at three things. You are also standing very {{b5}}. Even when the tram shakes.
Mateus: That is the second reason. If I move, an old woman next to me will move too. She does not want to move. If I am still, she is still. My body is her {{b6}}.
Chloe: Your body is her handrail. I have {{b7}} heard a job described like that.
Mateus: I learned it from my uncle. He worked on this tram for thirty-one years. He said, 'A good conductor is {{b8}} that thinks.'
Chloe: Furniture that thinks. I am going to write that {{b9}}.
Mateus: Write it down, but also try it. Stand still for one stop. You will feel how many people are leaning on {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: Chloe says she is a teacher.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mateus says, 'A good conductor is furniture that thinks.' Think of one person in your life — a colleague, a relative, a neighbor — whose job is mostly to be steady for others. What would change for those around them if that person 'moved' even a little?
